summary: Mutation testing results: 61 killed, 3 survived, 84 no coverage. Mutation score: 42% overall (96% on covered code). The 84 NoCoverage mutants span all async I/O functions (handlePush, buildFilePush, buildStdinPush, readNoTicketsDir, readStdin, loadPushConfig, runCli). The 3 surviving mutants are all in parseArgs.
testQuality: Tests cover parseArgs thoroughly for happy-path cases but miss three mutant-killing edge cases. The entire async I/O surface of cli.ts has zero test coverage, leaving 84 mutants alive. These functions contain the core business logic for the push command.

suggestions.description: NoCoverage: handlePush, buildFilePush, buildStdinPush, loadPushConfig, readNoTicketsDir, readStdin, and runCli (lines 26-140) have zero test coverage — 84 mutants are unreachable
suggestions.rationale: All async I/O logic for the push command is untested. This includes the projectId guard, dry-run branching, stdin vs file-based path selection, directory traversal logic, and the runCli dispatch switch.
suggestions.suggestion: Add unit tests for runCli and handlePush using vi.mock for node:fs/promises, detectAgent, createApiClient, buildPushAuth, assemblePush, and mergeSession. Key scenarios: (1) runCli(['push','--dry-run']) with NO_TICKETS_PROJECT_ID set asserts console.log called with JSON payload; (2) runCli(['push']) with env var missing asserts Error thrown containing 'NO_TICKETS_PROJECT_ID'; (3) runCli(['push','--stdin']) with mocked stdin asserts mergeSession called; (4) runCli(['help']) asserts usage string printed; (5) runCli(['init']) asserts process.exitCode = 1; (6) readNoTicketsDir with mixed .md and non-.md entries in both root and subdirectory asserts only .md files returned.

suggestions.description: Survived: args: [] mutated to args: ['Stryker was here'] when returning help for zero arguments
suggestions.rationale: The test 'parseArgs returns help for no arguments' only asserts result.command === 'help' but does not assert that args and flags are empty.
suggestions.suggestion: In the existing test 'returns help for no arguments', add: expect(result.args).toEqual([]); expect(result.flags).toEqual({});

suggestions.description: Survived: loop condition i < argv.length mutated to i <= argv.length — off-by-one goes undetected
suggestions.rationale: No test triggers an off-by-one past the end of argv. The guard on line 167 silently swallows the undefined element, so the loop boundary mutation goes undetected.
suggestions.suggestion: Add a test asserting the exact shape of parseArgs output including that args and flags have no extra entries. Example: expect(parseArgs(['push','--dry-run'])).toEqual({ command: 'push', args: [], flags: { 'dry-run': true } }). The deep equality check on the full object will fail if an extra undefined sneaks into args.

suggestions.description: Survived: if (!arg) continue mutated to if (false) continue — the falsy-arg guard is never exercised
suggestions.rationale: No test provides a falsy element in argv, so the guard is dead code under existing tests.
suggestions.suggestion: Either add a test passing a falsy element in argv — e.g. parseArgs(['push', undefined as unknown as string, '--dry-run']) — to verify the guard filters it and the result still has args: [] and flags: { 'dry-run': true }, or add a Stryker ignore comment to document the guard as intentionally defensive.
